title: Μύρμηξ καὶ περιστερά
source: Émile Chambry, Ésope: Fables, texte établi et traduit (Les Belles Lettres, Paris, 1927), fable 242 (= Perry 235). Text as printed, including Chambry's editorial punctuation (« », ⟨ ⟩ for editorial supplements). Pronunciation: reconstructed Attic, after W. S. Allen, Vox Graeca. Romanization: standard classical transliteration (ē/ō; th/ph/ch; y for υ, u in diphthongs; h = rough breathing; ēi/āi/ōi for iota subscript; accents omitted).

sentences.t: Μύρμηξ διψήσας, κατελθὼν εἰς πηγήν, παρασυρεὶς ὑπὸ τοῦ ῥεύματος ἀπεπνίγετο.
sentences.tr: Myrmēx dipsēsas, katelthōn eis pēgēn, parasyreis hypo tou rheumatos apepnigeto.
sentences.l: An ant, having-thirsted, having-gone-down to a spring, swept-away by the current, was-drowning.
sentences.i: An ant, feeling thirsty, went down to a spring, was swept away by the current, and began to drown.
sentences.n: Four verbs to disaster: three aorist participles stacked before the imperfect ἀπεπνίγετο, which holds him mid-drowning — the tense leaves the outcome open for the rescue.

sentences.t: Περιστερὰ δὲ τοῦτο θεασαμένη κλῶνα δένδρου περιελοῦσα εἰς τὴν πηγὴν ἔρριψεν, ἐφ' οὗ καὶ καθίσας ὁ μύρμηξ διεσώθη.
sentences.tr: Peristera de touto theasamenē klōna dendrou perielousa eis tēn pēgēn erripsen, eph' hou kai kathisas ho myrmēx diesōthē.
sentences.l: And a dove, this having-seen, a twig of-a-tree having-stripped-off, into the spring threw-it, on which indeed having-sat the ant came-through-safe.
sentences.i: A dove saw it, broke a twig from a tree and dropped it into the spring; the ant climbed aboard and was saved.

sentences.t: Ἰξευτὴς δέ τις μετὰ τοῦτο τοὺς καλάμους συνθεὶς ἐπὶ τὸ τὴν περιστερὰν συλλαβεῖν ᾔει.
sentences.tr: Ixeutēs de tis meta touto tous kalamous syntheis epi to tēn peristeran syllabein ēiei.
sentences.l: And a bird-limer certain after this, the reeds having-fitted-together, for the the dove catching was-going.
sentences.i: Soon after, a fowler fitted his limed reeds together and moved in to take the dove.
sentences.n: The ἰξευτής hunted with rods smeared in birdlime (ἰξός) — the κάλαμοι here are his tackle, not the riverside plant. ἐπὶ τό + infinitive: purpose again, 'with a view to catching.'

sentences.t: Τοῦτο δ' ὁ μύρμηξ ἑωρακὼς τὸν τοῦ ἰξευτοῦ πόδα ἔδακεν.
sentences.tr: Touto d' ho myrmēx heōrakōs ton tou ixeutou poda edaken.
sentences.l: But this the ant having-seen, the of-the fowler foot bit.
sentences.i: But the ant, who had seen it all, bit the fowler's foot.

sentences.t: Ὁ δὲ ἀλγήσας τούς τε καλάμους ἔρριψε καὶ τὴν περιστερὰν αὐτίκα φυγεῖν ἐποίησεν.
sentences.tr: Ho de algēsas tous te kalamous erripse kai tēn peristeran autika phygein epoiēsen.
sentences.l: And he, having-felt-pain, both the reeds threw-down and the dove at-once to-flee caused.
sentences.i: He flinched with pain, dropped his reeds, and so made the dove fly off at once.
sentences.n: ἐποίησεν + infinitive: 'made (her) flee' — causation in the plainest Greek. The rescue is repaid in strict symmetry: twig for the drowning ant, bite for the hunted dove.

sentences.t: Ὁ μῦθος δηλοῖ ὅτι δεῖ τοῖς εὐεργέταις χάριν ἀποδιδόναι.
sentences.tr: Ho mythos dēloi hoti dei tois euergetais charin apodidonai.
sentences.l: The fable shows that one-must to-the benefactors gratitude give-back.
sentences.i: The fable shows that kindness must be repaid.
sentences.n: χάριν ἀποδιδόναι — the exact phrase the mouse promised the lion (χάριτας ἀποδώσει). The two fables are twins: gratitude repaid across a difference in size.
